task: I need a simple way to clean up a growing pile of project files before it turns into a mess. Can you help me set up a folder and naming approach that makes it easier to find the right version later and keep exports straight?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it simple and rigid.

## Folder structure
Use one main project folder, then separate **working**, **exports**, and **archive**:

```text
Project_Name/
  01_Working/
  02_Media/
  03_Exports/
  04_Documents/
  05_Archive/
```

If you want it a little tighter for post work:

```text
Project_Name/
  01_Project_Files/
  02_Source_Media/
  03_Exports/
  04_Notes_QC/
  05_Archive/
```

## Naming rule
Use the same pattern everywhere:

```text
Project_Client_Description_YYYYMMDD_rev01
```

Example:
```text
Acme_InterviewCut_20260429_rev01
Acme_InterviewCut_20260429_rev02
```

For exports, make the version obvious:

```text
Project_Client_Description_YYYYMMDD_rev01_MASTER
Project_Client_Description_YYYYMMDD_rev01_H264
Project_Client_Description_YYYYMMDD_rev01_QC
```

## Simple rules to keep it clean
- **Never** overwrite a previous version
- Put **working files** and **final exports** in different folders
- Use **rev01, rev02, rev03** instead of “final_final”
- Add the **date** if you expect multiple versions over time
- Keep one folder for **approved exports only**
- Move old or risky files into **Archive**, don’t leave them in the active folder

## A practical setup
```text
Project_Name/
  01_Project_Files/
    Project_Name_rev01.prproj
    Project_Name_rev02.prproj
  02_Source_Media/
  03_Exports/
    WIP/
    Client_Review/
    Final/
  04_Notes_QC/
  05_Archive/
```

## Best habit
Before you make changes, save a new revision:
- duplicate the project
- rename it with the next rev number
- export into the matching export folder

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page folder template** you can copy for every project.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is basically the right shape. I’d want one more layer of discipline though — the naming needs to be a little more specific so it doesn’t get sloppy after a few revisions.

What I’d do:
- keep the project root dead simple
- separate **project files**, **source**, **exports**, **notes/QC**, **archive**
- use one revision convention everywhere

Something like:

```text
Project_Name/
  01_Project_Files/
  02_Source_Media/
  03_Exports/
    01_WIP/
    02_Client_Review/
    03_Final/
  04_Notes_QC/
  05_Archive/
```

For naming, I’d avoid stuffing too much into the filename unless you really need it. I’d go with:

```text
Client_Project_rev01_20260429
```

Then for exports:

```text
Client_Project_rev01_20260429_MASTER
Client_Project_rev01_20260429_H264
Client_Project_rev01_20260429_QC
```

A couple things I’d add:
- don’t mix date formats — pick one and keep it
- don’t use “final” unless it’s actually final-final
- if there are multiple deliverables, add a short suffix: `16x9`, `9x16`, `captioned`, whatever
- archive old review exports instead of leaving them in the active export folder

I’ve had the exact problem where everything starts out neat and then suddenly there are six versions of the same cut and nobody can tell which one got sent. The fix is mostly just being boring and consistent.

If you want, I can give you a tighter version for **premiere project folders specifically** — with autosaves, exports, and gfx all separated cleanly.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the key is to make it boring and consistent.

## Simple folder structure
Keep the root clean, then split by function:

```text
Project_Name/
  01_Project_Files/
  02_Source_Media/
  03_Exports/
    01_WIP/
    02_Client_Review/
    03_Final/
  04_Notes_QC/
  05_Archive/
```

## Naming convention
Use one pattern everywhere:

```text
Client_Project_rev01_20260429
```

For exports, add only what matters:

```text
Client_Project_rev01_20260429_MASTER
Client_Project_rev01_20260429_H264
Client_Project_rev01_20260429_QC
```

If needed, add a short deliverable tag:

```text
Client_Project_rev02_20260429_16x9
Client_Project_rev02_20260429_9x16
Client_Project_rev02_20260429_captioned
```

## Rules to keep it from getting sloppy
- Pick **one date format** and never change it
- Use **rev01, rev02, rev03** for project versions
- Don’t use “final” until it’s truly final
- Never overwrite a previous export
- Keep review exports out of the final folder
- Move old versions to **Archive** instead of leaving them active

## Best habit
Every time you make a meaningful change:
1. save a new project revision
2. export to the matching export folder
3. keep QC notes in one place
4. archive anything outdated

If you want, I can turn this into a **copy-paste template** for Premiere, Resolve, or general post folders.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s solid. The only thing I’d tighten is the revision rule so it doesn’t drift.

I’d use this:

```text
Client_Project_20260429_rev01
Client_Project_20260429_rev02
Client_Project_20260429_rev03
```

Then exports stay tied to that exact revision:

```text
Client_Project_20260429_rev03_MASTER
Client_Project_20260429_rev03_H264
Client_Project_20260429_rev03_9x16
```

That way the date marks the work period, and `rev` tells you the actual version. Cleaner than mixing the two in a weird way.

For the folders, your structure is the right one. I’d just keep the export subfolders strict:
- `01_WIP` = internal only
- `02_Client_Review` = anything sent out for notes
- `03_Final` = only approved deliverables

One extra thing I’d add: keep a tiny text file in `04_Notes_QC/` with the send history. Just simple stuff like:
- what was delivered
- when
- to whom
- which revision

That saves a lot of guessing later when somebody asks, “which file did we actually send?”

So yeah — this is the kind of system that works because it’s a little boring on purpose. That’s usually the point.
